mnemonics.title: TRS - Translational Rotational Scaling
mnemonics.content: TRS helps you remember the three basic 3D transformations: Translation (sliding), Rotation (spinning), and Scaling (resizing). Think: 'To Really Survive' - TRS

mnemonics.title: ROSE for Rotation
mnemonics.content: Remember rotation matrices using ROSE: Rx (Roll), Oz (not used), Sy (not used), Ex (not used) - actually use: 'ROtation Standard Equation' - cos for cos, -sin for sin in first row, sin for second row, cos for third row

mnemonics.title: Homogeneous H
mnemonics.content: Homogeneous coordinates add a 'H' (height/w) dimension: (x, y, z, w). For points w=1, for vectors w=0. Remember: 'Points have Position (1), Vectors have Direction (0)' - PPD

mnemonics.title: Pyramid Order for Composite Transforms
mnemonics.content: Remember the order: P' = T × R × S × P (Translate, then Rotate, then Scale). Think: 'Teen Raat Soti Pairo' (Hindi) - T R S P

mnemonics.title: SISS for Scaling Matrix
mnemonics.content: Scaling Matrix has 'SISS': ScaleX, 0, 0, 0, ScaleY, 0, 0, 0, ScaleZ, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1. Remember: 'Simply InScale Square Setup'

mnemonics.title: Translation Matrix - 1-2-3-4
mnemonics.content: Translation matrix has 1 at (0,0), 1 at (1,1), 1 at (2,2), 1 at (3,3), and tx, ty, tz in last column. Remember: 'Identity + translations' = 'I See T' (ICT)

acronyms.usage: The three fundamental linear transformations in 3D graphics. Always apply in this order: Scale → Rotate → Translate for correct composite transformations
Converting 3D point (x,y,z) to homogeneous (x,y,z,w) where w=1 for points. Used to represent all transformations as 4x4 matrix multiplications
The world coordinate system where all objects are defined. Transformations convert local/object coordinates to GCS
The object's own coordinate system. Transformations convert LCS to GCS through modeling transformations
The three main transformation pipelines in computer graphics: Model (object space to world), View (world to camera), Projection (camera to screen)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine sliding a book across a table - the object moves but doesn't rotate or change size. In matrix, the tx, ty, tz values directly add to x, y, z coordinates
Think of a clock face - the hour hand rotates around the center (origin). The X coordinate becomes Xcosθ - Ysinθ, Y becomes Xsinθ + Ycosθ
Imagine zooming in/out on an image. Uniform scaling multiplies all coordinates by same factor. Non-uniform scales different axes differently
Imagine tilting a stack of papers - the top moves while bottom stays fixed. X-shear shifts X based on Y value, making rectangles into parallelograms
Think of looking in a mirror. Reflection about XY plane keeps X,Y same but negates Z (z becomes -z). Matrix has -1 in the reflecting axis
Think of building a robot arm: first scale the joint, then rotate it, then move the entire arm to position. Matrix multiplication applies right-to-left in code (P' = T×R×S×P)
Picture a spreadsheet where top 3x3 handles rotation/scaling, last column handles translation, bottom row [0 0 0 1] is for homogeneous coordinates

analogies.analogy: Moving furniture in a room - you slide the sofa from one corner to another without rotating or resizing it
A ceiling fan spinning around its center - all points rotate around the origin (pivot point) by the same angle
Using zoom on Google Maps - making an object bigger or smaller while maintaining its proportions (uniform) or stretching it (non-uniform)
A deck of cards tilted to one side - the top cards shift relative to bottom cards, creating a slanted shape
Looking in a mirror - your reflection is the same but flipped. Reflection about XY plane is like seeing an object from below the floor
Following a recipe - you apply ingredients (transformations) in specific order. Adding sugar last vs first changes the final cake
Adding a denominator to fractions - just like ½ = 2/4, homogeneous coords (x,y,z,w) represent the same point as (x/w, y/w, z/w)
